title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Nina Patel, who designs apps for children.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm talking to Nina Patel, who designs apps for children. Nina, how did you get into this work? || Guest: I studied computer science, but I wasn't sure what kind of company to join. My sister was a primary school teacher, and she complained that many educational apps were dull. [20]I began making a simple spelling game for her class, and the children's reaction persuaded me to continue[/20]. They wanted to play it even during breaks, which was encouraging. || Interviewer: What is the biggest challenge when designing for children? || Guest: Adults often think bright colours are enough, but children lose interest quickly if instructions are confusing. [21]The hardest thing is making each task clear without filling the screen with too many words[/21]. Young users don't read long explanations. You need pictures, sound and very simple choices, but the activity must still feel like a game. || Interviewer: How do you test a new app? || Guest: We test with teachers, of course, but [22]watching children use the app is the most useful stage because they show problems immediately[/22]. They don't politely pretend something works. If a button is in the wrong place, they tap everywhere else first. I learn more from five minutes in a classroom than from a long report. || Interviewer: Do parents ever worry about children using screens? || Guest: Yes, and I understand that. I don't think an app should replace books, outdoor play or talking to adults. [23]I tell parents our apps are designed for short practice sessions, not for children to use for hours[/23]. A ten-minute game can help spelling, but it shouldn't become the whole afternoon. || Interviewer: What are you proudest of so far? || Guest: We made an app for children who find reading difficult. [24]I'm proud because several teachers told us quiet children started joining in more after using it[/24]. The app didn't magically solve everything, but it gave them a safe place to practise before reading aloud in class. That felt more important than sales numbers. || Interviewer: What are you working on now? || Guest: Our next app is about science. [25]It will let children do small experiments at home using ordinary things like paper, water and kitchen spoons[/25]. We want them to explore safely without needing expensive kits. The technology should guide them, but the real learning will happen away from the screen. || Interviewer: Do you think technology will keep changing education? || Guest: Yes, but I hope it changes in a thoughtful way. Schools don't need more screens just because screens look modern. They need tools that help teachers do something useful. When I visit classrooms, I always ask teachers what wastes their time or what pupils find difficult. Those answers are more important than any new technical idea. If an app makes a lesson calmer, clearer or more enjoyable, then it has a purpose. If it only looks clever, I would rather not build it. I also try to visit schools that do not have much money for technology. Their teachers are often the most inventive, and they remind me that a useful idea does not need to be complicated.  That is why I still spend time in classrooms every month.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm talking to Nina Patel, who designs apps for children. Nina, how did you get into this work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I studied computer science, but I wasn't sure what kind of company to join. My sister was a primary school teacher, and she complained that many educational apps were dull. I began making a simple spelling game for her class, and the children's reaction persuaded me to continue. They wanted to play it even during breaks, which was encouraging.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is the biggest challenge when designing for children?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Adults often think bright colours are enough, but children lose interest quickly if instructions are confusing. The hardest thing is making each task clear without filling the screen with too many words. Young users don't read long explanations. You need pictures, sound and very simple choices, but the activity must still feel like a game.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you test a new app?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We test with teachers, of course, but watching children use the app is the most useful stage because they show problems immediately. They don't politely pretend something works. If a button is in the wrong place, they tap everywhere else first. I learn more from five minutes in a classroom than from a long report.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do parents ever worry about children using screens?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, and I understand that. I don't think an app should replace books, outdoor play or talking to adults. I tell parents our apps are designed for short practice sessions, not for children to use for hours. A ten-minute game can help spelling, but it shouldn't become the whole afternoon.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are you proudest of so far?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We made an app for children who find reading difficult. I'm proud because several teachers told us quiet children started joining in more after using it. The app didn't magically solve everything, but it gave them a safe place to practise before reading aloud in class. That felt more important than sales numbers.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are you working on now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Our next app is about science. It will let children do small experiments at home using ordinary things like paper, water and kitchen spoons. We want them to explore safely without needing expensive kits. The technology should guide them, but the real learning will happen away from the screen.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you think technology will keep changing education?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but I hope it changes in a thoughtful way. Schools don't need more screens just because screens look modern. They need tools that help teachers do something useful. When I visit classrooms, I always ask teachers what wastes their time or what pupils find difficult. Those answers are more important than any new technical idea. If an app makes a lesson calmer, clearer or more enjoyable, then it has a purpose. If it only looks clever, I would rather not build it. I also try to visit schools that do not have much money for technology. Their teachers are often the most inventive, and they remind me that a useful idea does not need to be complicated.  That is why I still spend time in classrooms every month.

questions.question: Why did Nina first make an educational app?

options.A: Her university asked her to create one.
options.B: She wanted to join a large technology company.
options.C: Her sister needed something for her pupils.

questions.explanation: Nina says she began making a spelling game for her sister's class.

questions.question: What does Nina say is most difficult when designing apps for children?
questions.explanation: She says the hardest thing is making tasks clear without using too many words.

questions.question: What helps Nina improve an app most?
questions.explanation: Nina says watching children use the app is the most useful testing stage.

questions.question: What advice does Nina give parents?

options.A: Children should use educational apps every day.
options.B: Apps are better than some books for spelling.
options.C: Children should use the apps only for short periods.

questions.explanation: She tells parents the apps are designed for short practice sessions, not hours of use.

questions.question: Why is Nina proud of one reading app?

options.A: It helped some children take part more.
options.B: It won an important prize for design.
options.C: It sold better than the company expected.

questions.explanation: She says teachers told her quiet children started joining in more after using it.

questions.question: What will Nina's next app encourage children to do?
questions.explanation: The next app will guide children through experiments using ordinary things at home.
